Front: GitHub Actions: what triggers a workflow, the hierarchy, and what is a runner?
Back: An event triggers it via on: (push, pull_request, schedule/cron, workflow_dispatch for manual). Hierarchy: Workflow -> Jobs -> Steps. A runner is the machine/VM that executes a job (GitHub-hosted like ubuntu-latest, or self-hosted); fresh and ephemeral per job.

Front: GitHub Actions jobs: parallel or sequential, and do they share a filesystem?
Back: Jobs run in PARALLEL by default; serialize with needs: (job B needs job A). Each job gets its own isolated runner (different machine/filesystem), so you pass files between jobs via artifacts (upload-artifact/download-artifact). Steps WITHIN a job share the same runner filesystem; separate jobs do not.

Front: GitHub Actions: run vs uses in a step.
Back: run: executes shell commands on the runner (e.g. pip install, pytest, docker build). uses: pulls in a reusable published action by owner/repo@version (e.g. actions/checkout@v4 to get your code, actions/setup-python@v5, docker/build-push-action). Command you'd type -> run; reusable versioned building block -> uses. Checkout is a uses action, not run.

Front: GitHub Actions: how do you use a secret, and why the dedicated mechanism?
Back: Store it as a repository (or org/environment) secret, then reference ${{ secrets.NAME }} in the workflow. Dedicated mechanism because: the YAML lives in the repo + git history (a hardcoded secret is exposed forever, even after deletion), and GitHub encrypts secrets at rest and auto-masks them in logs (shown as ***). Encryption + masking + out-of-repo storage.

Front: What steps would a CI workflow run for a FastAPI repo on push?
Back: On a fresh ubuntu-latest runner: checkout (uses: actions/checkout) -> setup-python (uses: actions/setup-python) -> install deps (run: pip install -r requirements.txt) -> run tests (run: pytest) - the gate -> build the Docker image (optionally push). The test step is the whole point of CI; without it you're only building.
